words: SAM SCHRAGER: So ya just been sitting around, huh?
ARTHUR BJERKE: Oh, getting around, getting so dang stiff and stumbly that I
can't get around much.

speaker: SAM
words: Yeah, I wanted to go back and ask you some things, the way we was talking

speaker: SAM
words: last time, about the old days. How did your parents come about coming out

speaker: SAM
words: here and getting the place that they got?

speaker: AB
words: They homesteaded it. You know, we come here in 1890 to Genesee from Dakota.

speaker: AB
words: And dad worked on tha railroad when they built it through Troy. And he got

speaker: AB
words: acquainted with some people that homesteaded down here on Bear Creek, right

speaker: AB
words: down below Deary. So we moved up there, and then he come up here and he took

speaker: AB
words: this homestead, and we moved up here. And that's about all there is to it.

speaker: AB
words: And we moved on the place the 17th day of March, 1891. And our neighbor

speaker: AB
words: over here, Martin Olson, he moved on his place the 18th day of March, 1891.

speaker: AB
words: He stayed with us 'tils he got the cabin built over there.

speaker: SAM
words: Were there people here at that time?

speaker: AB
words: Oh there was a few. Well, Louie Halseth and M. S. Perry and them up

speaker: AB
words: around Helmer was here, and the Lawrences.

speaker: SAM
words: What was the land like then?

speaker: AB
words: Well It was pretty heavy timbered. But the Indians had kept it burnt off

speaker: AB
words: so it wasn't much underbrush. You could drive pret'near anywheres you wanted

speaker: AB
words: to in the open timber.

speaker: SAM
words: What did they do, your parents, to start homesteading here? What did they

speaker: SAM
words: do on the land?

speaker: AB
words: Well, they started in cleaning it up a little, and breaking up a little

speaker: AB
words: land. Then they slashed down a bunch of timber on the flats here, and

speaker: AB
words: burnt off the logs. It was all black pine. And then they grubbed out the

speaker: AB
words: brush roots that was, and then seeded it to timothy and cut hay with the

speaker: AB
words: scythe to keep a few cows.

speaker: SAM
words: Was it hard? Hard living? Was it hard getting by?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah. Well the first year, you know it was pretty hard to get any

speaker: AB
words: groceries or anything. We had a few chickens, and by gosh we lived pret'near

speaker: AB
words: on bread and eggs. And I got so that I believe it was 30 years before I'd

speaker: AB
words: eat an egg after that. And I don't care for an egg now. I can eat an egg,

speaker: AB
words: a time or two, and I don't want another egg again.

speaker: SAM
words: You got plenty sick of it.

speaker: AB
words: Well had a few chickens, and they laid awful good.

speaker: SAM
words: What do you remember from when you were a little boy?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, I can remember quite a few things all right. You know, I remember when

speaker: AB
words: we was in Dakota. We lived in the cellar back there, that is, in a sod

speaker: AB
words: shanty. And there was quite a lot of wolves back there. And I can remember

speaker: AB
words: my sister, she got up on the top of the roof on that sod shanty, and sit and

speaker: AB
words: barked at the wolves. And she was 97 years the day before yesterday; and

speaker: AB
words: she lives in Wenatchee. And then I remember when we come across^the mountains,

speaker: AB
words: there had been a train wreck, and there was kind of a lake there, and a car

speaker: AB
words: wreck, and spilled a bunch of cattle into the lake. They were laying out

speaker: AB
words: there floating in the water, I ,can remember that. And then I can remember

speaker: AB
words: when we got to Spokane, it was after the Spokane fire, and Spokane just had

speaker: AB
words: a rough lumber shack for a depot.

speaker: SAM
words: What do you remember from when you first come down here?

speaker: AB
words: Oh I can remember it was kind of a rough country. And then,our next

speaker: AB
words: neighbor up here that lived up on this place here, their name was Halseth.

speaker: AB
words: They had four boys. We went to the first school together. That was I and

speaker: AB
words: my sister, and the four Halseth boys, and Hawkin Peterson, and the three

speaker: AB
words: nigger kids, that was the first school kids. There was 10 of us. I can

speaker: AB
words: remember that.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you work around the house a lot when you were a boy?

speaker: AB
words: ; Oh yeah. Yeah, I kind of learnt, well, built on the house here a little.

speaker: AB
words: And after I got part grown up, a carpenter worked here, and I worked with

speaker: AB
words: him and I kind of learnt to be a kind of a carpenter. So after I growed up

speaker: AB
words: I done a lot of carpenter work around through the country. Yes, there is

speaker: AB
words: houses out on the ridge and in Deary and Bovill and Helmer that I've built.

speaker: SAM
words: You told me a story about a man that your father met who he had known a

speaker: SAM
words: long time ago. Will you tell me that story?

speaker: AB
words: Yeah. Well, dad went over from Norway to Sweden and went to work in the

speaker: AB
words: timber up there. And there was an English outfit that bought a bunch of

speaker: AB
words: timber in Sweden. Of course they'd have to log out to the small creeks.

speaker: AB
words: And the timber was shipped down to the ocean and loaded on the boat and shipped

speaker: AB
words: to England and sawed there. The biggest timber he took out up there and they

speaker: AB
words: sawed—and they made a lot of square timber—and the biggest square timber

speaker: AB
words: he took out was a 14 by 14, 76 feet long. That takes quite a stick of timber

speaker: AB
words: to make that kind of timber! Then, of course they couldn't log in the

speaker: AB
words: summer when it was dry; and then they worked around for the farmers there,

speaker: AB
words: and cleared land and one thing another, just to keep the horses busy. And

speaker: AB
words: he worked for a fella by the name Sahlin up there, and broke some land for

speaker: AB
words: him. He wasn't married and dad wasn't neither. And dad worked up there ten

speaker: AB
words: years, and he went back to Norway and he got married, and Sahlin is married

speaker: AB
words: a widow, and she had a boy, his name was Wallner. And they come to this

speaker: AB
words: country, and this Wallner started a dry goods store in Troy. Well, there

speaker: AB
words: was a lot of Swedes and Norweigans in Troy and around on this side, and of

speaker: AB
words: course they all went to Wallner's to trade. And dad was in there one day,

speaker: AB
words: and a Swede come in there, and he talks Swede to beat the dickens. And dad

speaker: AB
words: says to him, he says, "I hear you are from Jamtland,"

speaker: AB
words: The fella straightened up and he says, '.'Have you been in Oamtland?"

speaker: AB
words: Dad says, "Yes." He says he was out there 10 year. Well this fella

speaker: AB
words: wanted to know what he'd done out there. Well, dad says in the winters they

speaker: AB
words: worked for the Dixon Timber Company there, logging, and in the summer, when

speaker: AB
words: they couldn't log, they'd work for the farmers around there, clear land,

speaker: AB
words: break land, and one thing another. And this fella, he straightened up and

speaker: AB
words: he said, "It surely wasn't you that broke my plow?"

speaker: AB
words: Well, dad says, "I don't know whether I broke your plow or not," but he

speaker: AB
words: says, "I know I broke my own quite a few time." They got to talking, and by

speaker: AB
words: god dad has broke land for this Sahlin in Sweden, 40 years before,

speaker: AB
words: and they come and met here in Troy! Isn't that something?

speaker: SAM
words: It sure is.

speaker: AB
words: Well, Magnuson, he homesteaded down here, and then there was another fella

speaker: AB
words: by the name of Eric Larson that homesteaded down there. But then they couldn't

speaker: AB
words: get money, you know—like Eric Larson and Ben Lee—so they could file on

speaker: AB
words: that land at a certain time. And that's went back and been state land. But

speaker: AB
words: Magnuson bought that Eric Larson's land, so Ed Magnuson has got that. And

speaker: AB
words: he sold it to Carlson from down here by Troy, so they have got it now. And

speaker: AB
words: Eric Larson, he made a mistake. He come up here to hunt, and he slipped and

speaker: AB
words: fell on a log and shot himself accidently. He had them high cuffs with brass

speaker: AB
words: buttons in. Well it blowed off half of his arm and tore one of them buttons

speaker: AB
words: out of his cuff, and that hit him in the mustache and it stopped back and out

speaker: AB
words: of the eye. And that was a brass button, and that killed him.

speaker: AB
words: And then the Lawrences up here, at the old post office where Dansville

speaker: AB
words: was, they homesteaded up there about in the 90's. And Louie Halseth and

speaker: AB
words: M. S. Perry come in about '98. And Andrew Berg and the Erickson up there on

speaker: AB
words: the meadows, they come in about '89.

speaker: SAM
words: Did the different homesteaders visit each other very much?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah, they visited quite a lot together. By god they were more sociable

speaker: AB
words: then than they are now. By god you'd meet a neighbor on the road—there

speaker: AB
words: was all horse and wagon—they'd pull in under a shady spot somewheres and

speaker: AB
words: sit there and visit for an hour or so. And first I could remember—well hell,

speaker: AB
words: it was way up into the '30's you know—by god, pret'near every place pret'near

speaker: AB
words: every Saturday night, there would be a dance in some of the old cabins, and

speaker: AB
words: they'd all be there. And there was always somebody that could play. They'd

speaker: AB
words: play, and the women'd bring a lunch, and they'd have a big midnight lunch

speaker: AB
words: and maybe they'd dance 'til daylight and have a good time. But now, by god

speaker: AB
words: you don't see one another anymore.

speaker: AB
words: There was a fella down the other night—well it was the 13th he was

speaker: AB
words: down—that used to work for me on the thrashing machine. He worked for me

speaker: AB
words: about six years straight. And his sister was down. Well, this fella and

speaker: AB
words: his wife live down in Oregon around Salem somewheres, and his sister lives

speaker: AB
words: up here in this side of Deary. She fell here a year ago and broke her hip

speaker: AB
words: so she can't get around very good; she is pret'near my age. And they used

speaker: AB
words: to be along too on the parties. Well hell, there was parties all over. And

speaker: AB
words: even the niggers was along,

speaker: SAM
words: You mean Doe Wells and his children?

speaker: AB
words: Well, that is the younger part of them, that is Chuck and Mary and them.

speaker: AB
words: Roy didn't dance.

speaker: SAM
words: How well did those kids fit in? Did they get teased a lot about being

speaker: SAM
words: Negroes?

speaker: AB
words: No, hell no, they got along good with the white. And they associated with

speaker: AB
words: the white right along. Goddamit, if it was a party with the whites, the

speaker: AB
words: niggers was right along. Hell I've danced with them niggers lots of times.

speaker: AB
words: And by god they were good dancers too.

speaker: SAM
words: What was Joe Wells like? Do you remember?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, he was a pretty good size nigger. But his wife was pretty light colored.

speaker: AB
words: I think she was kind of a mulatto. Oh year, I remember them. You know I can

speaker: AB
words: remember the first time I seen them niggers, I was just a kid. Boy I was

speaker: AB
words: scared to death of them. But it didn't take me long to get over that, when

speaker: AB
words: we started to go to school.

speaker: SAM
words: Was there a lot of joking about them being Negroes instead of whites?

speaker: AB
words: No, not much. Old Doe, you know, he used to say he was the only white man

speaker: AB
words: up around here, the rest of them was Swedes. Old Doe he used to say, "I

speaker: AB
words: like my whiskey, but Lou likes her tabacco." Well, the second house they

speaker: AB
words: had up there, it was an old lumber floor, and knotholes had fell out of the

speaker: AB
words: lumber. And by god, you know, she'd chew tobacco, and she'd sit there, and

speaker: AB
words: by god she could spit through them knotholes just as good as anybody. Well

speaker: AB
words: finally she died, and of course Doe kept a-drinkin', and whiskey went out of

speaker: AB
words: fashion, so that it was all moonshine; and he got too much of that moonshine,

speaker: AB
words: and that killed him. He come out with the Wells boys from Carolina. And

speaker: AB
words: about the first settlers in around down below Deary was Carolinians. Well,

speaker: AB
words: really the first bunch that settled up in here was seven Germans, but they

speaker: AB
words: left pretty quick, biggest part of them. One of them was Mike Tebor and

speaker: AB
words: one, his name was Albert Riggs. And down below here, they started in calling

speaker: AB
words: this the Seven Devil's Country, on account of them seven Germans.

speaker: SAM
words: But they didn't stay.

speaker: AB
words: They didn't stay. They just stayed a year or two and they left.

speaker: SAM
words: Who were these South Carolinians?

speaker: AB
words: Well there was Frank Wells and Crom Wells, and a fella by the name of Cole,

speaker: AB
words: and Mark Rogers and Bert Rogers...

speaker: SAM
words: Well, you got most of 'em.

speaker: AB
words: And Charlie Burlinghouse, and Frank and Mace McCoy, and Frank Ellis and

speaker: AB
words: Terry Ellis and old Hinchley...

speaker: SAM
words: Did they all come out together from Carolina?

speaker: AB
words: They come pretty well all together.

speaker: SAM
words: So you think they knew each other back there?

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah, a lot of them did. Well, Cole and the Wells boys and Mark Rogers

speaker: AB
words: and them, they knew one another, they come right from the same place.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you know anything about the Indians that were here in the old days? Did

speaker: SAM
words: you used to see them?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, we used to see them quite a lot. They used to pass by here quite

speaker: AB
words: often. There used to be quite a lot of huckleberries here. And there always

speaker: AB
words: used to be a bunch of Indians stop up there. A lot of times they'd stop

speaker: AB
words: right down in the yard here and get water here. There was one Indian that

speaker: AB
words: come along one time, and he's broke his wagon tongue, and he stopped, and he

speaker: AB
words: wanted to know...

speaker: AB
words: (End' of Side A)

speaker: AB
words: ...And he wanted to know if I had any haywire, because he broke his wagon

speaker: AB
words: tongue. I took a look at it and I says, "Hell, you don't want any haywire

speaker: AB
words: for that." Because I've learnt so I was a pretty fair blacksmith. Well

speaker: AB
words: I went down in the shop, and I had a tongue just like it. So I took the

speaker: AB
words: irons off from that that I'd built, and put on it to brace it. And I took

speaker: AB
words: them up, and a couple, three, four bolts, and I drilled some holes; knocked

speaker: AB
words: the bolts out of the holes, and I put this iron on top, and put it on, and

speaker: AB
words: fixed his wagon tongue. It was a heavy hack he had. Well you know, I never

speaker: AB
words: been thanked so much in all of my life as I was from that Indian. Well

speaker: AB
words: there was years afterwards, every time he'd come by, he'd always stop, and

speaker: AB
words: he always wanted to know if I remember when I fixed the wagon tongue for him.

speaker: AB
words: There was a bunch of trees along the road out here, and they'd always stop

speaker: AB
words: there and have lunch.

speaker: AB
words: Oh yeah, they used to be quite a few Indians here. I remember, you

speaker: AB
words: know, they used to stop, well I was just a little bit of a kid, you know.

speaker: AB
words: And they were telling around that Indians stole kids, you know, God, when

speaker: AB
words: them Indians come along, you know, mother was scared to death that the

speaker: AB
words: Indians would steal me. Well I wasn't afraid of the Indians. The only

speaker: AB
words: ones I was afraid of was the nigger kids. I always wanted to get out with

speaker: AB
words: the Indians when they'd stop and have lunch outside!

speaker: AB
words: Well, when I growed up, I herded cattle for a fella down there by

speaker: AB
words: Moscow. He lived out on that catty-corner road that want across from Viola

speaker: AB
words: to Palouse. He was raised on the same place that dad was in Norway. And

speaker: AB
words: you know, there, they went by the name of the place; so he was the same

speaker: AB
words: name that dad was. His name was Ivor, and he had a brother by the name Henry;

speaker: AB
words: and there was one more of 'em, I can't think of his first name, and a horse

speaker: AB
words: kicked him to death. He had a few cattle, and he brought 'em up here, and

speaker: AB
words: I took caieof his cattle for a year or two. And then finally, him and Hooker

speaker: AB
words: was raised in the same place in Norway, they put their cattle together, so

speaker: AB
words: it made about 400 head. Well, I was about 16, 17. So they got me to herd

speaker: AB
words: them. And they had the Smith Meadow leased back of the Cherry Butte there.

speaker: AB
words: And those days, you know, you could keep the sheep off two miles from leased

speaker: AB
words: land, so they couldn't move in. We'd move the cattle up there in the spring.

speaker: AB
words: And then they had the Horse Ranch leased. And we'd keep 'em up there in

speaker: AB
words: the Smith Meadow for awhile; then we'd move up there on the Horse Ranch.

speaker: AB
words: Then they had the Shattuck's and Hughes' Meadow—at that big meadow on this

speaker: AB
words: side of Elk River where the road crosses the meadow there—they had that

speaker: AB
words: leased. So when we'd keep 'em on the Horse Ranch awhile, so the grass would

speaker: AB
words: get short, we'd move 'em over on the Shattuck's and Hughes Meadow, and keep

speaker: AB
words: 'em there 'tils it was pret'near time to move out.

speaker: AB
words: I was with the cattle all the time. Had a tent I lived in. I had a

speaker: AB
words: little black dog, and by god he was a dandy. And those days, you know, you

speaker: AB
words: could buy .22 cartridges for 10 cents a box, that is, the .22 short or three

speaker: AB
words: boxes for 25 cents. And .22 longs, 15 cents a box, or two for a quarter.

speaker: AB
words: Well all I was doing, I had a .22, I'd shoot and practice every day. Hell,

speaker: AB
words: I wouldn't think of shooting at a bird's body. If I couldn't shoot his head

speaker: AB
words: off from the horse's back I wasn't hitting. I'd take a bunch of .22 cartridges

speaker: AB
words: and set 'em up on 50 feet, and if I couldn't knock them empty shells off in

speaker: AB
words: 50 feet, nine out of ten, I wasn't shooting. Well Hooker's partner, he had

speaker: AB
words: a .22 Winchester, they were about a 25 caliber. So he kept at it up there

speaker: AB
words: at my camp; and every time he'd come up, every week or two, of course we'd

speaker: AB
words: have a shooting match, a penny a shot. But we never could get but a penny

speaker: AB
words: or two ahead, because he was a damn good shot too.

speaker: AB
words: Well that dog I had, I hardly took him with me in the woods. He'd

speaker: AB
words: stay right there at the camp, because there was quite a few bears around,

speaker: AB
words: and by god he'd keep the bear out of the camp. And that dog, he'd always

speaker: AB
words: lay right at the foot of my bed every night, and nothing could get in that

speaker: AB
words: camp. But he was a damn good cattle dog. And he was a good hunting dog

speaker: AB
words: too.

speaker: AB
words: Well, when we'd move the cattle, Andy'd come up. And I!d have them

speaker: AB
words: pretty well bunched, 50, 60, 70 head to the bunch, and I'd know pret'near

speaker: AB
words: where I had every cattle. And we'd gather 'em one day, and next day we'd

speaker: AB
words: move 'em. Well, we'd move 'em down to the Horse Ranch, and then next day

speaker: AB
words: we'd take 'em down here. Then we'd divide them here. Then maybe Hooker

speaker: AB
words: would be up, and Andy and Hooker'd take their cattle down that way. And

speaker: AB
words: Ivor Bjerke would come from down there, and he'd be up here, and we'd take

speaker: AB
words: his cattle to Moscow. I've drove a herd of cattle right down the main

speaker: AB
words: street in Moscow in the early day. They'd be surprised now if they'd see

speaker: AB
words: a herd of cattle coming down Main Street.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you ever have trouble with rustlers?

speaker: AB
words: No. I was up there on the Horse Ranch one time. I always had two horses.

speaker: AB
words: I used one, and I'd ride the other one. I'd get up just about four o'clock

speaker: AB
words: in the morning, and go around every bunch of cattle, and if some of 'em was

speaker: AB
words: a-gettin' too far back up, I'd turn 'em back, so they wouldn't get too far

speaker: AB
words: out. And one morning I saddled up a horse. And I haven't put up the rope;

speaker: AB
words: I just turned the horses loose with the long rope that they'd drag around

speaker: AB
words: in the field there, I generally had a chunk that they'd drag so they wouldn't

speaker: AB
words: go too far. This morning an Indian come along. He stopped. And he could

speaker: AB
words: talk pretty fair, and finally he says, "You want to trade horses?"

speaker: AB
words: "No, I don't want to trade horses."

speaker: AB
words: He set there quite a little while. He says, "Trade horses?"

speaker: AB
words: "No," I says, "I've got a pretty good horse." He had an awful nice

speaker: AB
words: looking horse. I had a kind of an old saddle on my horse, it was been used

speaker: AB
words: quite a lot.

speaker: AB
words: He set there a little and finally he says, "How you trade horses, saddle,

speaker: AB
words: the whole business?" Well he had a damn good saddle on the horse. I stood

speaker: AB
words: and I looked at him. I was just kind of a-wondering. I kind of thought

speaker: AB
words: maybe he stold that horse, but it was no brand on it. Finally I traded with

speaker: AB
words: him. He got off and and he get onto my horse. I get onto his horse. I

speaker: AB
words: rode out and the horse was all right. I made my trip and come in, and stopped

speaker: AB
words: awhile for lunch and went out again.

speaker: AB
words: That night I put a rope on him, and I tied him up to a stump out there

speaker: AB
words: on the meadow. Next morning I went out there, I thought I'd get him and

speaker: AB
words: saddle him. He acted pretty damn spooky. And I went to saddle him, and by

speaker: AB
words: god he throwed the saddle offa couple times before I got it cinched. Finally

speaker: AB
words: I got my saddle on. And those days I could ride pret'near anything that had

speaker: AB
words: hair on it. I got onto him and say boy, if you ever seen a horse that could

speaker: AB
words: buck, that sonofabitch did! Buck? But it didn't last long, when he seen

speaker: AB
words: he could throw me, he quit. But you know, I had to break him every morning.

speaker: AB
words: Well, there was a fella by the name Dosh Lamphear down there in the

speaker: AB
words: Hog Meadow. He was quite a horse trader. One day I rode the horse down

speaker: AB
words: there. I says to Dosh Lamphear. I says, "I've got a horse here I'd like

speaker: AB
words: to trade for a horse." I says, "He's kind of a mean horse to ride. I don't

speaker: AB
words: like one that's kind of mean to ride in the morning. Because a fella up

speaker: AB
words: there in the woods all alone, you never know what's liable to happen, you

speaker: AB
words: might get hurt, and nobody come along for a week or two."

speaker: AB
words: "Well," he says, "I got two, three horses here. I'll trade you for

speaker: AB
words: one of em." So finally I traded. I got a pretty fair looking horse from

speaker: AB
words: him. And the horse was nice and gentle. I put the saddle on the horse

speaker: AB
words: and went back up there. I got along fine with the horse.

speaker: AB
words: And it was only a week or two after that. This fella's nephew that

speaker: AB
words: lived over here, they kind of had an outlaw horse they couldn't drive worth

speaker: AB
words: a damn, he was balky. So he went up there and traded for a horse. He got

speaker: AB
words: this outlaw, and he wanted to know if they could break him all right. "Well,"

speaker: AB
words: Dosh says, "I don't know if you can ride him or not. Arthur drove him, rode

speaker: AB
words: him when he brought him in." And Ole went to get on the horse, and he

speaker: AB
words: got on the horse, and just as he landed on his back, he landed about 15 feet

speaker: AB
words: ahead of the horse in the road. (Laughs.) Well, he got up and they caught

speaker: AB
words: the horse again, and led the horse down. Hell, they couldn't no more drive

speaker: AB
words: that horse than they could fly! So finally they had take him up there, pay

speaker: AB
words: a little to boot and get another horse. And I don't know what Dosh ever done

speaker: AB
words: with him, because that Dosh was quite a horse trader.

speaker: SAM
words: Can you remember any other run-ins that you had with Indians?

speaker: AB
words: No, nothing else, just visited with »em. Visited with Mox Mox a few times.

speaker: AB
words: He was a pretty good Indian. Yeah, there was lot of horses down there—

speaker: AB
words: they'd go down and buy horses from him. Old Mox Mox was a good Indian.

speaker: AB
words: to He used to come up, and he didn't like see that they sold the timber to the

speaker: AB
words: company. "No," he says, "they spoil the country," which they did. Oh in the

speaker: AB
words: early days there used to be a lot of Indians come by here. But there ain't

speaker: AB
words: too many Indians left anymore.

speaker: SAM
words: What was Mox Mox like?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, he was a pretty tall Indian. He wasn't a bad looking Indian. And he

speaker: AB
words: could talk pretty good too. No, when they first started Bovill, you know,

speaker: AB
words: the town up there, old Mox Mox used to come up there quite a little. And he

speaker: AB
words: told Bovill that "They're spoiling the country now," he said. He wasn't

speaker: AB
words: coming back no more.

speaker: AB
words: Well when we settled out here, this flat over on this side used to be

speaker: AB
words: kind of a slough, and we drained it out; and when we went to break it out,

speaker: AB
words: we struck rocks once in awhile. There was quite a lot of flat rooks.

speaker: AB
words: They claim that the Indians has used them to grind camas for flour. Well

speaker: AB
words: one place down here, we struck a place, it was a bunch of rocks; they were

speaker: AB
words: built in a straight line and out that way and out that way about 30 inches

speaker: AB
words: wide, and about six and a half feet long, and you know, they were dug down

speaker: AB
words: in the ground that deep. We dug 'em out, and we kept a-lookin', and dad

speaker: AB
words: says, "I'll bet maybe we'll find some bones off froFm.an old Indian in here."

speaker: AB
words: But we never found none. But it's been something in way early days that the

speaker: AB
words: Indians has had there.

speaker: AB
words: And then it used to be places, round like that, that grass wouldn't

speaker: AB
words: grow. And right in the middle the grass would grow. And that spot'd be

speaker: AB
words: bare. I think it's been where there's been teepees and hides put down to

speaker: AB
words: sleep on, and it's killed the ground. I think that's what it's been. They

speaker: AB
words: all said it'd been war dances, but I don't think so. But you see lots of

speaker: AB
words: them places. Used to be a lot of 'em when we first come here, but now of

speaker: AB
words: course grass is growed over em.

speaker: SAM
words: So that's all that's left of the Indians here.

speaker: AB
words: Yeah. Oh, we used to find a few arrowheads once in awhile, and one thing

speaker: AB
words: another. We used to find quite a few of them but never took care of them.

speaker: AB
words: I'm kind of sorry of that. (Walks to a table:) Look at them. They claim

speaker: AB
words: the Indians used them as a mill grind camas with. I had quite a few of

speaker: AB
words: them out here, sitting out here, but they all disappeared, somebody'd pick

speaker: AB
words: them up.

speaker: SAM
words: Would you say it's about a foot long, maybe a little less, sharp pointed,

speaker: SAM
words: thick?

speaker: AB
words: (Picking up another piece:) That's quite a rock.

speaker: SAM
words: Crystal.

speaker: AB
words: (And another:) That's a kind of a flint, I believe.

speaker: AB
words: (End of Side B)

speaker: AB
words: I wish to Christ here five, six years ago that I would have took a summer

speaker: AB
words: off and went back to Denmark and Sweden and Norway. Because I could have

speaker: AB
words: spoke to all of them. Oh heck, I could read Swede just as good as I could

speaker: AB
words: Norweigan or American as far as that go. Our neighbor over here was a Swede.

speaker: AB
words: Well, dad was in Sweden for 10 years, you know. So he learned it, and he

speaker: AB
words: could talk just as good as any Swede, and he could read Swede. Well this

speaker: AB
words: fella over here, he had Swedish paper. When I was a kid I always went after

speaker: AB
words: the mail, so I'd bring his paper here. He had the Swedish-American Post.

speaker: AB
words: Well dad'd have to read that, and of course I'd read it, so I got so I could

speaker: AB
words: read it good. And Denmark and Norway, that used to be one country at one

speaker: AB
words: time, so they used the same language, but they pronounce different. But

speaker: AB
words: they used the same book, same Bible and everything. Well I could've went

speaker: AB
words: to Denmark, and then I could have went right across that little piece of the

speaker: AB
words: ocean that stick in there, and over to Malmo, Sweden. That used to belong

speaker: AB
words: to Denmark in the early day, but during the war Sweden took Skanor away from

speaker: AB
words: Demark, so it belongs to Sweden now.

speaker: SAM
words: Did your family, did they speak Norweigan when you were a boy at home?

speaker: SAM
words: Did you have to learn English in school?

speaker: AB
words: Why yes, they spoke at home, but the girls has learnt it on the outside,

speaker: AB
words: and when they were home, so I learnt it from them pretty well.

speaker: AB
words: Did your parents learn to speak English good?

speaker: AB
words: No, not very good. Pretty broken.

speaker: AB
words: Did it ever give them a hard time that they couldn't speak English?

speaker: AB
words: Oh, not too bad. Well dad, it didn't bother him so much. But mother, it

speaker: AB
words: bothered her a little. Well after dad sold the timber here, he bought 40

speaker: AB
words: acres down there on the ridge. And of course we raised quite a little hay

speaker: AB
words: here. And we baled the hay here, and that 40 acres was all cleared up,

speaker: AB
words: and there was a lot a hay down there. So we'd bring the cattle down there

speaker: AB
words: and keep them down there in the winter, and then we'd bring 'em up here and

speaker: AB
words: keep 'em here in the summer. Well, next to where we lived, there was a

speaker: AB
words: bunch of Germans around there. There was an old German, his name was

speaker: AB
words: Heck, he'd pret'near always come up to the place, every other night anyway.

speaker: AB
words: And he spoke awful broken English, so I learnt so I could get along pretty

speaker: AB
words: well with the German. So you could say there was four languages I could

speaker: AB
words: understand pretty good.

speaker: SAM
words: Can you tell me what your father was like? Tell me what kind of a guy

speaker: SAM
words: he was?

speaker: AB
words: Well that's his picture right there. When he was a kid, you know, there

speaker: AB
words: used to be a lot of them Russian wolves come over there, and I guess they

speaker: AB
words: were pretty vicious. That was in early days, you know, they used kind of

speaker: AB
words: them muzzle loaders and flintlocks. And they saved all the ashes out of

speaker: AB
words: stove and everything they could, and burnt potash out of it to make lye.

speaker: AB
words: Well, he was about 22, 21 or 2, and he was sitting up burning potash, and he

speaker: AB
words: heard the wolves. They had a hired man, and he got the hired man, and they

speaker: AB
words: went out to wolf hunt. They'd put a goat on the sled see, and them goddamn

speaker: AB
words: wolves would come right in on the sled. And dad put the swan screw in and

speaker: AB
words: pulled the charge out of the gun, because it's been laying out in the shop

speaker: AB
words: there where they were burning potash, so they figured it was maybe damp.

speaker: AB
words: And they poured in new powder, and put in a new flint, and they went out.

speaker: AB
words: And he's put in too much powder, and he blowed the gun to pieces, and the

speaker: AB
words: screw in the back end blowed out and went in under his eye and broke the

speaker: AB
words: cheekbone. I guess they operated on him six times. And those days they

speaker: AB
words: didn't use chloroform, you used to have to take it awake. They took out

speaker: AB
words: over 20 pieces of bone out of his face on that one side. That's why_ he's

speaker: AB
words: got the face turned so the picture is just from one side of the face.

speaker: SAM
words: Did you say that your mother had a harder time because she couldn't speak

speaker: SAM
words: English very well?

speaker: AB
words: Well, it bothered her more, but of course there was always somebody around.

speaker: AB
words: And dad got blood poison and gangrene in his legs, so he died. And got the

speaker: AB
words: banker from Deary to come out, he wanted his will made. They wanted to

speaker: AB
words: deed the place to me. Dad say, "If you'll take care of mother as long as

speaker: AB
words: she live, we'll deed the place to you."

speaker: AB
words: "Well," I says, "that's all right, but we'll see what the girls has

speaker: AB
words: got to say about that." Well the girls, they both said that they didn't

speaker: AB
words: want nothing, so they deeded the place to me. Then mother got what little

speaker: AB
words: money they had, and she had that.

speaker: AB
words: And when dad died, I and my sister went into town, you know, there was

speaker: AB
words: no undertaker around here. But the fella that had the store in there, he

speaker: AB
words: carried caskets and one thing another. He had several caskets in there.

speaker: AB
words: And we was upstairs looking at the caskets, and here he come up. And we

speaker: AB
words: was looking at one casket, it was a pretty nice looking casket, about the

speaker: AB
words: same as the rest of them was a-usin', 40 dollars for a casket those day.

speaker: AB
words: Well, he had one there that was marked 80 dollars. That was a nice casket.

speaker: AB
words: He come up, he says, "You don't want that'n, you want this'n over here."

speaker: AB
words: That was a bigger casket.

speaker: AB
words: "Yeah," I says, "a pretty big nice casket, and quite a lot of price."

speaker: AB
words: And you know, 40 dollars was a lot of money those days.

speaker: AB
words: "Well," he says, "you take that'n," he says, "you'll have that'n for

speaker: AB
words: 40 dollars." Well by god, you know, the neighbors around here, that've had

speaker: AB
words: people die and used that 40 dollars casket, so they went pret'near crazy

speaker: AB
words: over that, that I could buy that nice casket. I never told them that I paid

speaker: AB
words: only 40 for that.

speaker: SAM
words: Why do you think he gave it to you for 40?

speaker: AB
words: Well, I always traded with him. And he was kind of a funny fella. I

speaker: AB
words: don't know. Some people couldn't get along with him at all, and others again

speaker: AB
words: he got along with pretty good. There was another store in there that was

speaker: AB
words: kind of a-buckin' him too. But they couldn't do nothing with him because

speaker: AB
words: this fella that had the casket, by god he had the money! And he bought a

speaker: AB
words: lot of stuff by the carload lot, so he could sell cheaper than the rest of

speaker: AB
words: them, and make money. I know one time one fella brought in a bunch of hay

speaker: AB
words: wire, that is bailing wire, and he started in selling it cheaper than any

speaker: AB
words: wheres else. And by gosh old Dorgeson shipped in a carload, and by god he

speaker: AB
words: started in selling it for two and a half a spool, instead of three and a

speaker: AB
words: half at the other place. He was funny. But finally he sold out and he went

speaker: AB
words: down to Seattle.

speaker: AB
words: (He had two boys and two girls; and the oldest one of the girls died

speaker: AB
words: afterwards, but two of the boys I kind of think is alive, and one of the

speaker: AB
words: girls. They are down around Seattle. Well, the oldest boy come up here,

speaker: AB
words: oh it must be five, six years ago, and he come to Deary and visited 'em

speaker: AB
words: and by gosh nobody knew him. And one of the Andersons finally told them who

speaker: AB
words: he was. They come out to visit me. And I was a-workin' out here, and Victor

speaker: AB
words: says to me, he says "You know who this fella is?" I said, "Well just wait

speaker: AB
words: a minute, let me think." I looked at him and I said, "By god," I says, "he's

speaker: AB
words: got all the map of old U. C. Dorgeson all over his face." That was Dohnny

speaker: AB
words: Dorgeson. So we had quite a visit. Well, I ain't seen him for 40 years.)

speaker: SAM
words: I wanted to ask you what you did after you took care of the cattle. What

speaker: SAM
words: was the next job that you worked on after that?

speaker: AB
words: Well, I think about the next job I worked on then was around the place,

speaker: AB
words: and then when they started in building the railroad, I worked on the railroad

speaker: AB
words: gang some, driving team,

speaker: SAM
words: What do you remember about the building of that railroad?

speaker: AB
words: Well, I remember when they put the cut through in Deary there. They had

speaker: AB
words: a big kind of a steam outfit, kind of a plow, and they plowed the hardpan.

speaker: AB
words: And then they had a rig that run right alongside of it that plowed the dirt

speaker: AB
words: up on a kind of a scraper. And that went up, and the dirt fell in dump

speaker: AB
words: wagons, then they'd haul it out and dump it. They claim, that fill through

speaker: AB
words: Deary, that there is a dago buried in that fill there. And they claim there

speaker: AB
words: is another one buried in that fill up there at Cornell. You know, there

speaker: AB
words: was mostly dagos and that stuff on the shovel gang. And one'd get in that

speaker: AB
words: the rest of 'em didn't like, they'd hit him in the head, and just bury him

speaker: AB
words: in the dirt. Then cover him up,

speaker: SAM
words: Did you know about that back then, when they were building the railroad,

speaker: SAM
words: that there were a couple of guys missing?

speaker: AB
words: Well the bosses knew it all right, but what in hell did they care? Maybe

speaker: AB
words: these fellas had a couple, three hundred dollars coming in railroad work.

speaker: AB
words: Well, them fellas missing, they didn't have to pay 'em. See, that was

speaker: AB
words: money in the contractor's pocket.

speaker: AB
words: And when I drove team up there at Cornell, they blasted the whole damn

speaker: AB
words: cut there. It was wide, they were going to put in side tracks in there. And

speaker: AB
words: they had dump cars that run out with the dirt. And then they kind of had a

speaker: AB
words: bridge there we'd drive up on, and the dump cars run in under it, and a

speaker: AB
words: square hole; and we'd dump them slip scrapers so the dirt would go into the

speaker: AB
words: dump car. And when you'd get a dump car full, just start it, and then a fella

speaker: AB
words: would ride out on it, and brake it to a stop. Then they'd empty it, empty

speaker: AB
words: the car, and straighten up the car, and then a horse'd bring it back.

speaker: AB
words: One morning it was cold, and the fellas out there at the dump a kind

speaker: AB
words: of a-levelin', they were cold. They went down and set a fire to a big brushpile.

speaker: AB
words: Got it burning pretty good, and they got up onto the dump to dump a

speaker: AB
words: car that's come out. And by god some of the fellas that had been shooting

speaker: AB
words: left a box of powder in that brushpile. And by god that went off, and boy,

speaker: AB
words: there were sticks and logs and everything lying all over the country! Well

speaker: AB
words: there was a white man out there and a dago on the dump, boy they sure went

speaker: AB
words: a-runnin'. But nobody got hurt.

speaker: AB
words: Well I worked there 'til they got through with the team work. And I

speaker: AB
words: come home and stayed home awhile. There were a-buildin' a trestle, they

speaker: AB
words: called it High Grouse Trestle. So I went over there. They got up pretty

speaker: AB
words: high with it. And it didn't make any difference to me how high I went, I

speaker: AB
words: didn't get dizzy. The boss says, "Do you think you can stand it to walk on

speaker: AB
words: them timbers up there?" And that trestle was 97 feet high, you know. Well

speaker: AB
words: I got up there and I run back and forth on the timbers up there, and it didn't

speaker: AB
words: bother me. And so I got a job all right there, so I stayed there about a

speaker: AB
words: month during the winter. There was a fella from the old country that stayed

speaker: AB
words: here with the folks, and he done the chores here. Helped the folks do the

speaker: AB
words: chores, so I didn't have to stay at home.

speaker: AB
words: Well then, when we got through up there, the boss says, he says, "I

speaker: AB
words: wish to god," he says, "we had some more trestles to build." He says, "I'd

speaker: AB
words: like to have you went along, because you are about the fastest man on the

speaker: AB
words: trestle I've had." The rest of them were older man, and they were slower

speaker: AB
words: and more careful. Well, you know, them square timbers, they'd have to be

speaker: AB
words: bored and bolted together. And you take getting 97 feet high walking on

speaker: AB
words: 2 by 12's. You take, the stringers was 2 by 12's bolted together, so they'd

speaker: AB
words: make 12 by 12's. And you are a long ways up in the air when you get up

speaker: AB
words: there 90 feet to run across on them.

speaker: SAM
words: Do you remember what the dago's where like? Did you have much to do with

speaker: SAM
words: them?

speaker: AB
words: Well, they were Italians and Bohemians. Oh, some of them was damn nice

speaker: AB
words: fellas. There was one outfit staying up here that always got eggs an butter

speaker: AB
words: and meat from us. The boss's name was Pedro. He went back to Italy. And

speaker: AB
words: hell there's quite a few of them Italians that stayed here. You can't tell

speaker: AB
words: they're Italians now anymore. Hell they were just, some of 'em in one part

speaker: AB
words: of Italy, you know, some of them Italians was just as light as any of 'em.

speaker: AB
words: But the south part of Italy, they were pretty dark. And there was on

speaker: AB
words: Bohemian that used to live up there on the Hog Meadow, he turned out to be

speaker: AB
words: quite a logger. By god, you know, he was always making a little moaey;

speaker: AB
words: And by gosh, you know, when it got right hard times, quite a few fellas would

speaker: AB
words: go up there and work for him. And he always seemed to be able to have a

speaker: AB
words: little money. Well he had a nephew there that was kind of half foolish, and

speaker: AB
words: by god, you know, when he died...

speaker: AB
words: Transcribed and typed by Sherrie Fields
